description: Monitors Photon service and OpenSearch health, including Jetty threads and connections, JVM heap and non-heap memory, GC pauses and allocation, thread states and live threads, CPU usage, system load and open files, and OpenSearch shards, search rate and latency, index sizes, and cluster health.

panels.description: Total number of HTTP requests received by Photon across all endpoints during the selected time range. Useful for understanding overall traffic volume and spotting sudden spikes or drops.
panels.title: Total Requests (Time Range)

panels.description: Number of successful forward geocoding requests to /api — converting a search string (e.g. a place name or address) into geographic coordinates. Green = healthy traffic.
panels.title: Geocode (/api) Requests

panels.description: Number of successful reverse geocoding requests to /reverse — converting a lat/lon coordinate pair into a human-readable address or place name. This is Photon's primary use case.
panels.title: Reverse Geocode (/reverse) Requests

panels.description: Count of 4xx client error responses. A low baseline (e.g. malformed queries) is normal. A sudden spike may indicate a broken client, a bad deployment, or unexpected input formats.
panels.title: Error Requests (4xx)

panels.description: Total number of documents indexed in OpenSearch under the 'photon' index. Each document represents a geocodable place (street, POI, city, etc.). Drops here indicate data loss.
panels.title: OpenSearch Documents

panels.description: How long the Photon JVM process has been running without a restart. A low value may indicate a recent crash or planned restart.
panels.title: Uptime

panels.description: Request throughput per second broken down by endpoint (/api, /reverse, /status) and HTTP status code. Use this to distinguish traffic patterns between geocoding and reverse geocoding and identify error surges.
panels.title: Request Rate by Endpoint & Status

panels.description: Latency percentiles for forward geocoding (/api) requests. p50 is the median experience; p95/p99 reveal tail latency. Values above 500ms may indicate OpenSearch query slowness or JVM pressure.
panels.title: Request Latency avg / max (/api)

panels.description: Latency percentiles for reverse geocoding (/reverse) requests. Since reverse lookups typically use a geo_distance query in OpenSearch, latency here directly reflects OpenSearch responsiveness.
panels.title: Request Latency avg / max (/reverse)

panels.description: Rate of 4xx client error responses over time by endpoint and status code. 400s often mean invalid query parameters; 404s may indicate unknown routes. Sustained elevation warrants investigation.
panels.title: Error Rate (4xx)

panels.description: JVM heap memory usage by pool (Eden, Survivor, Old Gen). A sawtooth pattern is normal (GC cycles). A rising trend in Old Gen without drops may indicate a memory leak.
panels.title: JVM Heap Memory

panels.description: Non-heap memory used by the JVM — primarily Metaspace (class metadata) and CodeHeap (JIT-compiled code). These grow at startup and stabilize; unexpected growth may indicate classloader leaks.
panels.title: JVM Non-Heap Memory (Metaspace / CodeHeap)

panels.description: Time the JVM spends in garbage collection as a fraction of wall time. High GC pause rates reduce Photon's effective throughput. Frequent major GC pauses suggest heap is undersized.
panels.title: GC Pause Rate (Duration & Frequency)

panels.description: Rate at which the JVM allocates objects in the young generation (Eden). High allocation rates cause frequent minor GCs. 'Promoted' bytes show how much survives into Old Gen — a key indicator of GC pressure.
panels.title: GC Memory Allocation Rate

panels.description: JVM thread states stacked over time. 'RUNNABLE' threads are doing work; 'WAITING'/'TIMED_WAITING' are blocked on I/O or locks (e.g. waiting on OpenSearch). A high 'BLOCKED' count signals thread contention.
panels.title: JVM Thread States

panels.description: Total live and daemon thread counts plus the historical peak. A steadily increasing live thread count without a corresponding increase in load may indicate a thread leak.
panels.title: JVM Live Threads

panels.description: CPU utilisation of the Photon JVM process and the overall system. High process CPU with low system CPU suggests compute-bound geocoding queries. High system CPU may indicate OS-level I/O pressure.
panels.title: CPU Usage

panels.description: 1-minute system load average (number of runnable processes waiting for CPU) and the count of open file descriptors. A high open-file count relative to the OS limit will cause 'Too many open files' errors.
panels.title: System Load Average (1m) & File Descriptors

panels.description: OpenSearch shard distribution. Active shards serve queries; unassigned shards (shown in orange) indicate cluster health problems — Photon queries will fail or degrade until shards are reassigned.
panels.title: OpenSearch Cluster Shards

panels.description: OpenSearch query throughput and average latency per query. Latency spikes here directly translate into higher Photon response times, since each geocode/reverse-geocode triggers one or more OpenSearch searches.
panels.title: OpenSearch Search Rate & Latency

panels.description: Size of the OpenSearch 'photon' index on disk over time. Sudden drops indicate index truncation or deletion. Growth after a data import is expected; unexpected growth may indicate segment bloat.
panels.title: OpenSearch Index Size

panels.description: Current total disk footprint of the 'photon' OpenSearch index. Use this to track storage consumption and plan capacity. A single-node Photon setup typically has no replicas, so this equals raw data size.
panels.title: OpenSearch Index Size (Total)

panels.description: Memory used by JVM NIO direct buffers and mapped buffers. Photon's OpenSearch client uses direct buffers for network I/O. Persistent growth may indicate buffer pool exhaustion.
panels.title: JVM Buffer Memory Used

panels.description: Number of allocated JVM buffers by pool type. A sudden increase in buffer count can accompany network I/O storms or misconfigured connection pool sizes.
panels.title: JVM Buffer Count

title: Photon
